assessment.summary: A present, quantified global toll — over a million deaths a year directly attributable to resistant bacterial infections — with a thin pipeline of new antibiotics and rising resistance rates threatening to erode modern medicine's ability to treat common infections and perform routine procedures safely.
assessment.timeframe: Already a present, quantified toll; WHO and partners project resistance to last-resort antibiotics could roughly double by 2035 versus 2005 levels absent stronger action.

claims.text: Bacterial AMR was directly responsible for 1.27 million global deaths in 2019 and contributed to 4.95 million deaths.
AMR could result in US$ 1 trillion additional healthcare costs by 2050, and US$ 1 trillion to US$ 3.4 trillion gross domestic product (GDP) losses per year by 2030.
There are only 27 antibiotics in clinical development that address WHO bacterial priority pathogens, of which only 6 were classified as innovative.

description: The evolution of bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites to resist the drugs designed to kill them, driven by overuse and misuse of antimicrobials in humans, animals, and agriculture. Already a leading cause of death worldwide, it threatens to undermine modern medicine — from routine infections to surgery, chemotherapy, and organ transplants — as fewer effective antibiotics remain in the pipeline.
name: Antimicrobial Resistance
